title: Git rebase rewrites commit hashes — never rebase shared branches
body:
Rebasing replaces every commit with a new one (new SHA). If you rebase a branch that others are working on, their local copies diverge and they must force-pull.

```bash
# SAFE: rebase your local feature branch onto main
git checkout feature
git rebase main

# DANGEROUS: rebase a shared branch
git checkout main
git rebase feature  # rewrites main history

# If you accidentally rebased a shared branch:
git reflog  # find the pre-rebase commit
git reset --hard HEAD@{2}  # go back
git push --force-with-lease  # safer than --force
```

Rule of thumb: only rebase branches that no one else has pulled. If in doubt, use `merge` instead. `--force-with-lease` is safer than `--force` because it fails if someone else pushed in the meantime.

title: Git stash does not include untracked files by default
body:
Running `git stash` only stashes tracked, modified files. New untracked files are left behind, which can cause confusion when switching branches.

```bash
# Only stashes tracked changes
git stash

# Include untracked files
git stash -u
# or
git stash --include-untracked

# Include everything, even ignored files
git stash -a
# or
git stash --all

# Named stash for easier identification
git stash push -u -m "WIP: auth feature login form"

# List stashes
git stash list
# stash@{0}: On feature: WIP: auth feature login form

# Apply and drop
git stash pop  # apply most recent and remove from stash
git stash apply stash@{1}  # apply specific stash, keep in list
```

Always use `stash -u` if you have new files. Otherwise you'll switch branches, wonder where your new files went, switch back, and find them still sitting there unstashed.

title: Git cherry-pick creates a NEW commit, not a reference
body:
Cherry-picking copies a commit's changes into a new commit with a different SHA. The original and cherry-picked commits are unrelated in Git's history, which can cause duplicate changes during later merges.

```bash
# Cherry-pick a single commit
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Cherry-pick without committing (stage changes only)
git cherry-pick --no-commit abc1234

# Cherry-pick a range of commits
git cherry-pick abc1234..def5678  # exclusive start
git cherry-pick abc1234^..def5678  # inclusive start

# If cherry-pick conflicts:
git cherry-pick --continue  # after resolving conflicts
git cherry-pick --abort     # cancel the cherry-pick
```

If you cherry-pick commit X from branch A into branch B, then later merge A into B, Git may auto-resolve the duplicate, or it may conflict. To avoid this, prefer rebasing or merging over cherry-picking when possible.

title: Git submodules pin to a specific commit, not a branch
body:
Submodules record a specific commit SHA, not a branch reference. Pulling the parent repo doesn't automatically update submodule code. Team members must run `git submodule update` after pulling.

```bash
# Clone repo with submodules
git clone --recurse-submodules <url>

# If already cloned without submodules
git submodule update --init --recursive

# Update submodule to latest commit on its tracked branch
cd my-submodule
git checkout main
git pull
cd ..
git add my-submodule
git commit -m "Update submodule to latest"

# Or update all submodules at once
git submodule update --remote --merge
```

Common mistake: you update a submodule locally, commit the parent, push, and teammates see a dirty submodule. They need to run `git submodule update --init --recursive` after pulling. Consider adding this to a `post-merge` git hook.

title: Git hooks are not pushed with the repository
body:
The `.git/hooks/` directory is local to each clone. Hooks you write there are not shared with teammates when they clone the repo. Use a tool to manage shared hooks.

```bash
# Option 1: Configure a hooks directory in the repo
mkdir .githooks
# Add hooks to .githooks/pre-commit, .githooks/pre-push, etc.
git config core.hooksPath .githooks
# Teammates must run the same git config command

# Option 2: Use Husky (most popular for JS/TS projects)
npx husky init
# Creates .husky/ directory with hook scripts
# Installs automatically via prepare script in package.json

# .husky/pre-commit
npx lint-staged

# .husky/pre-push
npm test
```

```json
// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "prepare": "husky"
  },
  "lint-staged": {
    "*.{ts,tsx}": ["eslint --fix", "prettier --write"],
    "*.{json,md}": ["prettier --write"]
  }
}
```

Husky's `prepare` script runs on `npm install`, so new teammates get hooks automatically. Without a tool like Husky, everyone must manually set up their hooks.

title: Git LFS requires setup on every clone, not just the original repo
body:
Installing Git LFS and tracking files in `.gitattributes` isn't enough — every developer who clones the repo must also have Git LFS installed, or they get text pointer files instead of actual binaries.

```bash
# Initial setup (once per repo)
git lfs install
git lfs track "*.psd"
git lfs track "*.png"
git lfs track "*.zip"
git lfs track "*.woff2"
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Configure Git LFS for binary files"

# .gitattributes (auto-generated by git lfs track)
*.psd filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text
*.png filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text
*.zip filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text
```

```bash
# Clone a repo with LFS files
git lfs install  # must run before clone, or run after and then:
git lfs pull     # download actual file contents

# Check what LFS is tracking
git lfs ls-files

# Check for files that should be in LFS but aren't
git lfs migrate info
```

If a teammate sees garbled text files starting with `version https://git-lfs.github.com/spec/v1`, they need to run `git lfs install && git lfs pull`.

title: Git bisect requires a clean working tree or stashed changes
body:
Running `git bisect` with uncommitted changes causes checkout failures. Stash or commit your work before starting a bisect session.

```bash
# Stash changes first
git stash -u

# Start bisect
git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD          # current commit is broken
git bisect good v1.0.0       # this tag/commit was working

# Git checks out a middle commit. Test it, then:
git bisect good  # if this commit works
git bisect bad   # if this commit is broken
# Repeat until Git finds the first bad commit

# Automate with a test script
git bisect start HEAD v1.0.0
git bisect run npm test
# Git runs the script at each step, marks good/bad based on exit code

# When done
git bisect reset
git stash pop
```

The automated `bisect run` approach is powerful — write a script that exits 0 for good and non-zero for bad. It finds the exact commit that introduced a bug in O(log n) steps.

title: GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch inputs are always strings
body:
Even if you define an input as `type: boolean` or `type: number` in a workflow_dispatch trigger, the value is always passed as a string in the workflow context.

```yaml
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      deploy:
        description: 'Deploy after build'
        type: boolean
        default: false
      replicas:
        description: 'Number of replicas'
        type: number
        default: 1

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # BAD: comparing boolean input directly
      - if: ${{ inputs.deploy }}  # always true! it's the string "false"

      # GOOD: compare as string
      - if: ${{ inputs.deploy == 'true' }}

      # Or use fromJSON to convert
      - if: ${{ fromJSON(inputs.deploy) }}

      # Numbers need conversion too
      - run: echo "Replicas: ${{ inputs.replicas }}"
        # Works for echo, but for arithmetic:
      - run: echo "Double: $(( ${{ inputs.replicas }} * 2 ))"
```

The `fromJSON()` function is the cleanest way to handle boolean inputs. For numbers, shell arithmetic works since the string is numeric.

title: Git worktrees share the same stash and branch namespace
body:
Worktrees give you multiple working directories from a single repo, but stashes and branches are shared. You can't have the same branch checked out in two worktrees simultaneously.

```bash
# Create a worktree for a hotfix
git worktree add ../hotfix-123 -b hotfix/123

# Create a worktree for reviewing a PR
git worktree add ../pr-review origin/feature-branch

# List worktrees
git worktree list
# /Users/me/project          abc1234 [main]
# /Users/me/hotfix-123       def5678 [hotfix/123]

# Remove a worktree when done
git worktree remove ../hotfix-123
# Or just delete the directory and prune:
rm -rf ../hotfix-123
git worktree prune
```

Worktrees are ideal for: reviewing PRs without stashing, running tests on one branch while coding on another, and maintaining a production hotfix workflow. They're much lighter than a second clone because they share the `.git` object store.

title: GitHub branch protection rules don't apply to admins by default
body:
By default, repository admins can bypass branch protection rules (force push, push without PR, skip required checks). This is a security gap if admin accounts are compromised.

```yaml
# In GitHub UI: Settings > Branches > Branch protection rule
# Check: "Do not allow bypassing the above settings"
# This applies protection rules to everyone, including admins

# Or via GitHub API:
curl -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO/branches/main/protection \
  -d '{
    "required_status_checks": {
      "strict": true,
      "contexts": ["ci/test", "ci/lint"]
    },
    "enforce_admins": true,
    "required_pull_request_reviews": {
      "required_approving_review_count": 1,
      "dismiss_stale_reviews": true
    },
    "restrictions": null
  }'
```

Also enable: "Require branches to be up to date before merging" to prevent merging stale PRs. And "Require conversation resolution before merging" to ensure all review comments are addressed.

title: Git merge --squash doesn't create a merge commit
body:
`git merge --squash` stages all changes from the source branch as a single set of changes but doesn't commit. It also doesn't record the merge relationship, so Git doesn't know the branches were merged.

```bash
# Regular merge: creates merge commit, preserves history
git merge feature
# a--b--c--d (main)
#     \   /
#      e-f (feature)

# Squash merge: all changes as one commit, no merge relationship
git merge --squash feature
git commit -m "Add login feature (#42)"
# a--b--c--d--S (main, S contains e+f changes)
#     \
#      e-f (feature, still exists, not "merged" in git's eyes)
```

After a squash merge, the feature branch still shows as "unmerged" in git. Delete it immediately to avoid confusion:

```bash
git merge --squash feature
git commit -m "Add login feature"
git branch -D feature  # delete local
git push origin --delete feature  # delete remote
```

If you try to merge the same branch again later, you'll get conflicts because Git doesn't know those changes were already applied.

title: Git reflog is local only and expires after 90 days
body:
The reflog tracks every HEAD movement locally. It's your safety net for recovering lost commits, but it's not pushed to remotes and entries expire.

```bash
# View reflog
git reflog
# abc1234 HEAD@{0}: commit: add feature
# def5678 HEAD@{1}: checkout: moving from main to feature
# ghi9012 HEAD@{2}: commit: previous work

# Recover a commit after accidental reset
git reset --hard HEAD~3  # oops, lost 3 commits
git reflog  # find the commit hash
git reset --hard abc1234  # recover

# Recover a deleted branch
git branch -D feature  # oops
git reflog  # find the last commit on feature
git checkout -b feature abc1234  # recreate

# Change expiry (default: 90 days for reachable, 30 for unreachable)
git config gc.reflogExpire 180.days
git config gc.reflogExpireUnreachable 90.days
```

Reflog only exists in your local clone. If you delete and re-clone, reflog is gone. For critical repos, consider backing up the `.git` directory periodically. `git gc` can also prune reflog entries if run aggressively.

title: GitHub Actions secrets are not available in pull requests from forks
body:
For security, GitHub Actions does not expose repository secrets to workflows triggered by pull requests from forked repos. This breaks CI for open source projects that need API keys for tests.

```yaml
# This step will have empty secrets for fork PRs
- name: Run integration tests
  env:
    API_KEY: ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}  # empty string for fork PRs
  run: npm run test:integration

# Fix: conditionally skip secrets-dependent steps
- name: Run integration tests
  if: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name == github.repository }}
  env:
    API_KEY: ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}
  run: npm run test:integration

# Alternative: use pull_request_target (runs in base repo context)
on:
  pull_request_target:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
# WARNING: pull_request_target has access to secrets but runs
# untrusted code — only use for specific, sandboxed steps
```

The safe pattern for fork PRs: run unit tests (no secrets needed) on `pull_request`, and run integration tests on `pull_request_target` with explicit checkout of the PR code in a sandboxed step.

title: Git .gitattributes line endings can cause phantom diffs
body:
Without `.gitattributes`, Git may convert line endings inconsistently across platforms, causing files to show as modified even when content hasn't changed.

```bash
# .gitattributes — add to every repo
* text=auto eol=lf

# Force specific line endings for certain files
*.sh text eol=lf
*.bat text eol=crlf
*.ps1 text eol=crlf

# Binary files — never convert
*.png binary
*.jpg binary
*.ico binary
*.woff2 binary
*.ttf binary

# Lock files — exact content matters
package-lock.json -diff
yarn.lock -diff
pnpm-lock.yaml -diff
```

If you're already seeing phantom diffs from line endings:

```bash
# Fix all line endings in the repo
git add --renormalize .
git commit -m "Normalize line endings"
```

The `text=auto eol=lf` setting tells Git to normalize all text files to LF in the repo and check them out as LF on all platforms. This prevents the Windows CRLF vs Unix LF problem entirely.

title: GitHub CODEOWNERS file must be in specific locations to work
body:
GitHub only reads the CODEOWNERS file from three specific paths. If placed elsewhere, it's silently ignored and no reviewers are auto-assigned.

```bash
# Valid locations (checked in this order):
.github/CODEOWNERS
CODEOWNERS
docs/CODEOWNERS
```

```bash
# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Default owners for everything
* @team-lead

# Frontend
/src/components/ @frontend-team
/src/styles/ @frontend-team @design-team
*.tsx @frontend-team

# Backend
/src/api/ @backend-team
/src/db/ @backend-team @dba-team

# DevOps
/.github/workflows/ @devops-team
Dockerfile @devops-team
*.yml @devops-team

# Docs — anyone can approve
/docs/ @docs-team

# Security-sensitive
/src/auth/ @security-team @team-lead
.env.example @security-team
```

Rules are matched bottom-to-top — the last matching pattern wins. Teams must have read access to the repo. Individual users must be collaborators. Enable "Require review from Code Owners" in branch protection to enforce.

title: Git interactive rebase can lose commits if you delete lines
body:
In an interactive rebase, each line in the todo list represents a commit. Deleting a line drops that commit permanently (recoverable only via reflog).

```bash
# Interactive rebase last 5 commits
git rebase -i HEAD~5

# The editor shows:
pick abc1234 Add feature A
pick def5678 Fix typo
pick ghi9012 Add feature B
pick jkl3456 WIP save
pick mno7890 Final polish

# Common operations:
# reword — change commit message
# squash — combine with previous commit
# fixup — like squash but discard this message
# drop — remove this commit entirely
# edit — pause to amend this commit

# Example: clean up before merging
reword abc1234 Add feature A
fixup def5678 Fix typo
pick ghi9012 Add feature B
drop jkl3456 WIP save
fixup mno7890 Final polish
```

Safety tip: before an interactive rebase, create a backup branch: `git branch backup-feature`. If the rebase goes wrong, you can `git reset --hard backup-feature`.

title: GitHub Actions matrix strategy runs all combinations by default
body:
A matrix with multiple dimensions creates the Cartesian product of all values. Three OS options times three Node versions equals nine jobs, which can be expensive.

```yaml
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest]
        node: [18, 20, 22]
        # This creates 9 jobs (3 * 3)
      fail-fast: false  # don't cancel other jobs if one fails

    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node }}

  # Better: test all node versions on Linux, only latest on other OS
  test-optimized:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest]
        node: [18, 20, 22]
        include:
          - os: windows-latest
            node: 22
          - os: macos-latest
            node: 22
        # This creates 5 jobs instead of 9
```

Use `include` to add specific combinations and `exclude` to remove them. Set `fail-fast: false` for comprehensive testing, `fail-fast: true` (default) for faster feedback on failures.

title: Git reset --soft vs --mixed vs --hard: data loss differences
body:
The three reset modes undo different levels of work. Only `--hard` destroys uncommitted changes.

```bash
# --soft: undo commit, keep changes staged
git reset --soft HEAD~1
# Working dir: unchanged
# Staging area: contains the commit's changes
# Use case: rewrite the commit message, combine with more changes

# --mixed (default): undo commit, unstage changes
git reset HEAD~1
# Working dir: contains the changes (unstaged)
# Staging area: clean
# Use case: re-organize what goes into which commit

# --hard: undo commit AND discard all changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1
# Working dir: matches HEAD~1 exactly
# Staging area: clean
# Use case: completely abandon recent work
# ⚠️ DESTROYS uncommitted work

# Safe alternative to --hard for uncommitted files:
git stash -u  # save work first
git reset --hard HEAD~1
# If you need it back: git stash pop
```

Rule: never use `--hard` without checking `git stash list` and `git status` first. If you do lose work, check `git reflog` — committed changes are recoverable for ~30 days.

title: GitHub Actions checkout only fetches a single commit by default
body:
The `actions/checkout` action does a shallow clone (`--depth=1`) by default. This breaks tools that need git history like changelogs, version calculation, and git diff.

```yaml
# Default: shallow clone (depth 1)
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
# git log shows only 1 commit

# Full history
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0  # all history

# Specific depth (for git diff against recent commits)
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
  with:
    fetch-depth: 50

# Common issue: git diff fails
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- run: git diff HEAD~5  # fails! only 1 commit in history

# Fix for PR diff:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0
- run: git diff origin/main...HEAD
```

Tools affected by shallow clones: `semantic-release`, `changesets`, `git-cliff`, `git bisect`, `git blame`, and any custom script that reads commit history.

title: Git force-push-with-lease can still lose others' work in one case
body:
`--force-with-lease` is safer than `--force` but has a subtle gap: if you ran `git fetch` recently, the lease is based on the fetched state, not the actual remote state. If someone pushed between your fetch and your push, their work is lost.

```bash
# Safe in most cases:
git push --force-with-lease

# Vulnerable sequence:
git fetch  # updates remote tracking branch
# Someone else pushes to the branch
git rebase main
git push --force-with-lease  # succeeds! Overwrites their push
# Because --force-with-lease checks against your local remote-tracking ref

# Safest option: specify the expected value
git push --force-with-lease=origin/feature:abc1234
# Only succeeds if remote is exactly at abc1234

# Or use --force-if-includes (Git 2.30+)
git push --force-with-lease --force-if-includes
# Verifies that the remote ref has been integrated into the local branch
```

`--force-if-includes` is the most paranoid option — it checks that you've actually incorporated the remote changes into your local history before allowing the force push.

title: GitHub PR templates are only used for new PRs, not existing ones
body:
PR templates auto-fill the description when creating a new PR. They don't update existing PRs, and the template file must be in the right location.

```bash
# Valid locations for PR template:
.github/pull_request_template.md
.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md
pull_request_template.md
docs/pull_request_template.md

# Multiple templates (user picks via query parameter):
.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/
├── feature.md
├── bugfix.md
└── release.md
# Use: github.com/owner/repo/compare/main...feature?template=bugfix.md
```

```markdown
<!-- .github/pull_request_template.md -->
## What
<!-- Describe the change -->

## Why
<!-- Explain the motivation -->

## How to test
- [ ] Step 1
- [ ] Step 2

## Checklist
- [ ] Tests added/updated
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] No breaking changes (or documented in CHANGELOG)

## Screenshots
<!-- If applicable -->
```

If the template isn't appearing, check: file is on the default branch, file is in one of the valid locations, file has `.md` extension.

title: Git merge conflicts in package-lock.json should be regenerated, not resolved manually
body:
Never manually resolve conflicts in lock files. The file structure is machine-generated and manual edits create inconsistencies.

```bash
# When package-lock.json has merge conflicts:

# Option 1: Accept either version and regenerate
git checkout --theirs package-lock.json  # or --ours
npm install
git add package-lock.json
git commit

# Option 2: Delete and regenerate
git checkout --theirs package.json  # resolve package.json first
rm package-lock.json
npm install
git add package-lock.json package.json
git commit

# For yarn.lock:
git checkout --theirs yarn.lock
yarn install
git add yarn.lock

# For pnpm-lock.yaml:
git checkout --theirs pnpm-lock.yaml
pnpm install
git add pnpm-lock.yaml
```

Pro tip: use `npm install --package-lock-only` to regenerate the lock file without actually installing node_modules (faster). Add lock file merge strategy to `.gitattributes`:

```
package-lock.json merge=ours
```

title: GitHub Actions env context differs between workflow and step levels
body:
Environment variables set at the workflow level, job level, and step level have different scopes and can shadow each other.

```yaml
env:
  NODE_ENV: production  # workflow level

jobs:
  build:
    env:
      NODE_ENV: test  # job level — overrides workflow level
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: echo $NODE_ENV  # "test"

      - env:
          NODE_ENV: development  # step level — overrides job level
        run: echo $NODE_ENV  # "development"

      - run: echo $NODE_ENV  # back to "test" (step env doesn't persist)

      # Dynamic env from step output
      - id: version
        run: echo "value=1.2.3" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - env:
          APP_VERSION: ${{ steps.version.outputs.value }}
        run: echo $APP_VERSION  # "1.2.3"

      # Set env for all subsequent steps
      - run: echo "DEPLOY_URL=https://staging.example.com" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      - run: echo $DEPLOY_URL  # available in all subsequent steps
```

`$GITHUB_ENV` persists for all subsequent steps in the job. `$GITHUB_OUTPUT` makes values available to other steps via the `steps` context. Neither crosses job boundaries.

title: Git clean -fd deletes untracked files permanently without recovery
body:
Unlike `git reset` which can be undone via reflog, `git clean` permanently deletes untracked files. There's no recovery mechanism.

```bash
# See what would be deleted (always do this first)
git clean -n   # dry run for untracked files
git clean -nd  # dry run including directories

# Delete untracked files
git clean -f   # files only
git clean -fd  # files and directories
git clean -fdx # files, directories, AND ignored files (.env, node_modules, etc.)

# Interactive mode (safest)
git clean -i
# Shows a menu to selectively delete files
```

The `-x` flag is especially dangerous — it deletes `.env` files, build caches, `node_modules`, and anything in `.gitignore`. Always run with `-n` first. If you need a clean slate, consider `git stash -u` instead — at least you can recover with `git stash pop`.

title: GitHub Actions concurrency group cancels in-progress runs by default
body:
Setting a concurrency group without `cancel-in-progress: false` means new pushes cancel the currently running workflow. This can leave deployments in a half-finished state.

```yaml
# Dangerous for deployments: new push cancels mid-deploy
concurrency:
  group: deploy-${{ github.ref }}
  # cancel-in-progress defaults to false, but many templates set it to true

# Safe for CI (tests): cancel old runs on new push
concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true  # saves runner minutes

# Safe for deploys: queue instead of cancel
concurrency:
  group: deploy-production
  cancel-in-progress: false  # wait for current deploy to finish

# Per-PR concurrency (most common pattern)
concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

Use `cancel-in-progress: true` for CI (tests, linting) where you only care about the latest push. Use `cancel-in-progress: false` for deployments and release workflows.

title: Git commit --amend changes the commit hash even if only the message changes
body:
Amending a commit creates a new commit object with a new SHA, even if you only change the message. This means the old commit becomes unreachable (orphaned) and the branch pointer moves to the new commit.

```bash
# Amend the last commit message
git commit --amend -m "Better commit message"
# New SHA generated — old one is in reflog

# Amend to add forgotten files
git add forgotten-file.ts
git commit --amend --no-edit  # keep same message

# After amending a pushed commit, you must force push
git push --force-with-lease
# If others pulled the old commit, they'll get conflicts
```

Never amend commits that have been pushed to a shared branch. If you need to fix a pushed commit, create a new commit instead. Amending is only safe for local commits that haven't been shared.

title: GitHub Actions cache is scoped to the branch and has a 10GB limit
body:
Caches are isolated by branch. Feature branches can read the main branch's cache but not other feature branches' caches. The total cache storage per repo is 10GB, and least recently used caches are evicted.

```yaml
# Cache node_modules based on lockfile
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: node_modules
    key: node-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
    restore-keys: |
      node-${{ runner.os }}-

# Better: use setup-node built-in caching
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: 22
    cache: 'npm'  # or 'yarn', 'pnpm'

# Multiple cache paths
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: |
      node_modules
      ~/.cache/playwright
      .next/cache
    key: deps-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
```

Cache restore order: exact key match first, then `restore-keys` prefix matches (newest first). The `restore-keys` fallback is important — without it, a cache miss means starting from scratch.

title: Git diff shows nothing when changes are already staged
body:
`git diff` only shows unstaged changes. If you've already run `git add`, the diff appears empty, which confuses developers into thinking their changes are lost.

```bash
# Shows unstaged changes (working dir vs staging area)
git diff

# Shows staged changes (staging area vs last commit)
git diff --staged
# or equivalent:
git diff --cached

# Shows ALL changes (working dir vs last commit)
git diff HEAD

# Compare with a specific branch
git diff main  # all changes vs main
git diff main...HEAD  # changes since branching from main

# Show diff with stat summary
git diff --stat
git diff --stat main

# Show diff for specific file
git diff -- path/to/file.ts
git diff --staged -- path/to/file.ts
```

The triple-dot `...` syntax in `git diff main...HEAD` shows only changes introduced on the current branch since it diverged from main. This is what GitHub shows in PRs.

title: Git error: cannot rebase — you have unstaged changes
body:
Rebase refuses to start if you have uncommitted changes because it needs to checkout different commits during the rebase process.

```bash
# Error: Cannot rebase: You have unstaged changes.
# Error: Please commit or stash them.

# Fix 1: Stash changes
git stash -u
git rebase main
git stash pop

# Fix 2: Commit as WIP
git add -A
git commit -m "WIP"
git rebase main
# Later, undo the WIP commit:
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Fix 3: Use autostash (auto-stashes before rebase, pops after)
git rebase --autostash main

# Make autostash the default
git config --global rebase.autoStash true
```

Setting `rebase.autoStash true` globally is recommended. It automatically stashes your changes before rebase and re-applies them after, eliminating this error entirely.

title: Git error: refusing to merge unrelated histories
body:
This error occurs when merging two branches that share no common ancestor, typically when combining two repos or when a repo was re-initialized.

```bash
# Error: fatal: refusing to merge unrelated histories

# Fix: allow unrelated histories
git pull origin main --allow-unrelated-histories
# or
git merge other-branch --allow-unrelated-histories

# Common scenarios:
# 1. You initialized a new repo locally AND on GitHub with different commits
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git remote add origin <url>  # GitHub repo has its own initial commit
git pull origin main --allow-unrelated-histories

# 2. Combining two repositories
git remote add other-repo <url>
git fetch other-repo
git merge other-repo/main --allow-unrelated-histories

# Prevention: don't initialize GitHub repos with README/license
# if you plan to push an existing local repo
```

title: Git error: failed to push — remote contains work you don't have locally
body:
This happens when someone else pushed commits to the remote branch that you haven't pulled yet.

```bash
# Error: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you
# do not have locally.

# Fix 1: Pull then push (creates a merge commit)
git pull origin main
git push origin main

# Fix 2: Pull with rebase (cleaner history)
git pull --rebase origin main
# Resolve any conflicts, then:
git push origin main

# Fix 3: Fetch and rebase separately (more control)
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main
git push origin main

# Make pull --rebase the default
git config --global pull.rebase true
```

Never "fix" this with `git push --force` on a shared branch — that overwrites the other person's work. The only exception is feature branches where you're the sole contributor.

title: Git error: pathspec 'file' did not match any files known to git
body:
This error has several causes: the file doesn't exist, it's gitignored, or the path contains special characters.

```bash
# Error: error: pathspec 'src/utils/helper.ts' did not match any file(s) known to git

# Cause 1: File doesn't exist (typo in path)
ls src/utils/helper.ts  # check if file exists

# Cause 2: File is gitignored
git status --ignored  # shows ignored files
git check-ignore -v src/utils/helper.ts  # shows which gitignore rule matches

# Force-add an ignored file
git add -f src/utils/helper.ts

# Cause 3: Path has spaces or special characters
git add "src/my folder/file.ts"  # quote the path

# Cause 4: File exists only on another branch
git checkout feature -- src/utils/helper.ts  # get file from another branch

# Cause 5: Case sensitivity (macOS is case-insensitive, Linux is not)
git mv src/Utils/Helper.ts src/utils/helper.ts
```

On macOS, `Git` and `git` are the same directory. When you push to a case-sensitive Linux CI server, paths may not match. Use `git mv` to rename with correct casing.

title: GitHub Actions error: Resource not accessible by integration
body:
This permissions error means the workflow's `GITHUB_TOKEN` doesn't have the required permission. Default permissions were tightened in newer repos.

```yaml
# Fix: explicitly set permissions at the job or workflow level
jobs:
  deploy:
    permissions:
      contents: write     # push commits, create releases
      pull-requests: write # comment on PRs
      packages: write      # publish to GitHub Packages
      issues: write        # create/update issues
      id-token: write      # OIDC for cloud deployments
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: |
          # These operations now work:
          gh pr comment $PR_NUMBER --body "Deploy complete"
          gh release create v1.0.0
```

In repo settings > Actions > General, "Workflow permissions" controls the default. "Read repository contents" is the minimum default. For security, prefer explicit per-job permissions over changing the repo-wide default to read-write.

title: Git error: CONFLICT (modify/delete) during rebase or merge
body:
This conflict type means one side modified a file that the other side deleted. Git can't automatically resolve this.

```bash
# During merge/rebase:
# CONFLICT (modify/delete): src/old-component.tsx deleted in main
# and modified in feature. Version feature of src/old-component.tsx
# left in tree.

# Option 1: Keep the file (accept the modification)
git add src/old-component.tsx
git rebase --continue

# Option 2: Delete the file (accept the deletion)
git rm src/old-component.tsx
git rebase --continue

# Option 3: During merge
git add src/old-component.tsx  # or git rm
git commit  # completes the merge

# Prevention: communicate with your team about file deletions.
# If you know a file is being refactored/moved, coordinate.
```

This is common during large refactors where one branch renames/deletes files while another branch adds features to them. Frequent rebases from main reduce the scope of these conflicts.

title: GitHub Actions error: The workflow is not valid — no steps defined
body:
YAML formatting errors in GitHub Actions workflows produce cryptic error messages. The most common cause is incorrect indentation.

```yaml
# BAD: steps not indented under job
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:  # wrong indent — this is a sibling of build, not a child
    - run: echo hello

# GOOD: steps indented correctly
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:  # correct — child of build
      - run: echo hello

# BAD: using tabs instead of spaces
jobs:
	build:  # YAML doesn't allow tabs for indentation

# BAD: missing 'on' trigger
name: CI
jobs:  # no 'on:' section — workflow won't trigger
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

# GOOD: complete minimal workflow
name: CI
on: push
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm test
```

Use the GitHub Actions VS Code extension for syntax validation. Or validate workflows locally with `actionlint` — it catches most errors before you push.

title: Git error: detached HEAD state after checking out a commit or tag
body:
Checking out a commit hash or tag puts you in detached HEAD state. Any commits you make here are orphaned when you switch branches.

```bash
# Causes:
git checkout abc1234      # checkout a commit
git checkout v1.0.0       # checkout a tag
git checkout origin/main  # checkout remote tracking branch

# Warning message:
# You are in 'detached HEAD' state. You can look around, make
# experimental changes and commit them...

# If you made commits in detached HEAD and want to keep them:
git checkout -b my-new-branch  # create a branch at current position

# If you already left detached HEAD and lost commits:
git reflog  # find the orphaned commit
git checkout -b recovery abc1234

# To checkout a tag as a branch:
git checkout -b release-v1 v1.0.0  # creates branch from tag

# Return to a named branch:
git checkout main
```

Detached HEAD is useful for temporarily examining old code, but never commit important work there without creating a branch first.

title: Git error: pre-commit hook failed — preventing commit
body:
Pre-commit hooks run before each commit. If they exit with a non-zero code, the commit is blocked.

```bash
# Error: husky - pre-commit hook exited with code 1

# See what the hook does
cat .husky/pre-commit
# or
cat .git/hooks/pre-commit

# Common causes:
# 1. Lint errors
npx eslint --fix src/  # fix lint errors
git add -A  # re-stage fixed files
git commit -m "Your message"  # try again

# 2. Type errors
npx tsc --noEmit  # check types

# 3. Test failures
npm test  # run tests

# Skip hook (ONLY if you know what you're doing)
git commit --no-verify -m "WIP: skip hooks"
# or
GIT_SKIP_HOOKS=1 git commit -m "WIP"

# Debug the hook
bash -x .husky/pre-commit  # run with debug output
```

Don't make `--no-verify` a habit. If a hook is consistently failing on valid code, fix the hook configuration rather than bypassing it.

title: Git error: unable to access — SSL certificate problem
body:
SSL certificate errors when pushing/pulling from HTTPS remotes. Common behind corporate proxies or with self-signed certificates.

```bash
# Error: SSL certificate problem: unable to get local issuer certificate

# Fix 1: Update CA certificates
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install ca-certificates  # Linux
brew install ca-certificates  # macOS

# Fix 2: Point Git to the correct CA bundle
git config --global http.sslCAInfo /path/to/ca-bundle.crt

# Fix 3: Corporate proxy — add proxy certificate
git config --global http.sslCAInfo /path/to/corporate-ca.pem

# Fix 4 (NOT recommended for production): disable SSL verification
git config --global http.sslVerify false
# Only use for testing. Re-enable with:
git config --global http.sslVerify true

# Fix 5: Use SSH instead of HTTPS
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:owner/repo.git
```

Disabling SSL verification is a security risk. Prefer adding the correct CA certificate or switching to SSH authentication.

title: GitHub Actions error: HttpError — rate limit exceeded for API calls
body:
GitHub API rate limits apply to `GITHUB_TOKEN` operations in Actions. The default rate is 1,000 requests per hour per repository.

```yaml
# BAD: calling GitHub API in a loop without rate limit handling
- run: |
    for pr in $(gh pr list --json number -q '.[].number'); do
      gh pr review $pr --approve  # each call uses API quota
    done

# GOOD: batch operations where possible
- run: |
    gh api graphql -f query='
      query {
        repository(owner: "owner", name: "repo") {
          pullRequests(first: 100, states: OPEN) {
            nodes { number title }
          }
        }
      }'
    # Single GraphQL query instead of N REST calls

# Handle rate limits in scripts
- run: |
    response=$(gh api /rate_limit)
    remaining=$(echo $response | jq '.resources.core.remaining')
    if [ "$remaining" -lt 10 ]; then
      echo "Rate limit low: $remaining remaining"
      reset=$(echo $response | jq '.resources.core.reset')
      sleep_until=$((reset - $(date +%s)))
      echo "Waiting ${sleep_until}s for rate limit reset"
      sleep $sleep_until
    fi
```

Use GitHub's GraphQL API to batch requests. A single GraphQL query can replace dozens of REST calls. Also consider using a GitHub App token which has higher rate limits (5,000 requests/hour).

title: Git error: Your local changes would be overwritten by checkout
body:
Git refuses to switch branches if doing so would overwrite uncommitted changes to files that differ between the branches.

```bash
# Error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout:
#   src/config.ts
# Please commit your changes or stash them before you switch branches.

# Fix 1: Stash changes
git stash -u
git checkout other-branch
git stash pop  # after you're done on the other branch

# Fix 2: Commit first
git add -A
git commit -m "WIP: save before switching"
git checkout other-branch

# Fix 3: Force checkout (DESTROYS uncommitted changes to those files)
git checkout -f other-branch  # dangerous

# Fix 4: Use worktrees instead of switching
git worktree add ../other-branch-dir other-branch
cd ../other-branch-dir
# Work there, then come back
```

This error only occurs when the files you've modified also differ between the current and target branches. If the files are the same on both branches, Git carries your uncommitted changes with you.

title: GitHub Actions error: context access might be invalid — secrets in if conditions
body:
You can't use `secrets` directly in `if` conditions because they're masked and treated as empty strings in boolean context. Use a workaround to check if a secret is set.

```yaml
# BAD: secret is always empty in if
- name: Deploy
  if: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY != '' }}  # always false!
  run: deploy.sh

# GOOD: set secret as env var, check the env var
- name: Check deploy key
  id: check
  run: echo "has_key=${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY != '' }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  # This works because the expression is evaluated before masking

- name: Deploy
  if: steps.check.outputs.has_key == 'true'
  env:
    DEPLOY_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}
  run: deploy.sh

# Alternative: use a separate non-secret variable as a flag
# In repo settings > Variables:
# DEPLOY_ENABLED = true
- name: Deploy
  if: ${{ vars.DEPLOY_ENABLED == 'true' }}
  env:
    DEPLOY_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}
  run: deploy.sh
```

The `secrets` context is intentionally restricted in conditional expressions to prevent leaking secret values through workflow logs.

title: Git error: fatal: bad object HEAD after repo corruption
body:
A corrupted HEAD reference usually means the `.git` directory was damaged (disk error, interrupted operation, or storage issues).

```bash
# Error: fatal: bad object HEAD
# Error: fatal: your current branch appears to be broken

# Fix 1: Reset HEAD to a known good commit
git reflog  # might work if reflog is intact
git reset --hard <known-good-commit>

# Fix 2: Rebuild HEAD from remote
git fetch origin
cat .git/refs/remotes/origin/main  # get the latest commit hash
git update-ref HEAD <hash>

# Fix 3: Check and repair
git fsck --full  # find dangling/corrupt objects
git gc --aggressive  # garbage collect and repack

# Fix 4: Nuclear option — re-clone
mv .git .git-backup
git clone <url> temp-clone
mv temp-clone/.git .
rm -rf temp-clone
git checkout -- .  # restore working directory
```

Prevention: don't kill Git operations mid-way (especially during rebase or gc). Use a reliable filesystem. Consider `git maintenance start` for periodic maintenance tasks.

title: GitHub Actions error: Process completed with exit code 1 — generic failure
body:
This generic error means a shell command returned a non-zero exit code. Debugging requires enabling verbose output and checking the specific command that failed.

```yaml
# BAD: no error details
- run: npm run build
# Error: Process completed with exit code 1

# GOOD: enable verbose output
- run: npm run build
  env:
    CI: true
    DEBUG: '*'  # enable debug logging for most tools

# Or use set -x for shell debugging
- run: |
    set -euxo pipefail
    npm run build
    # -e: exit on error
    # -u: error on undefined variables
    # -x: print each command before execution
    # -o pipefail: fail on pipe errors

# Check for common silent failures
- run: |
    # Node ran out of memory (common for large builds)
    NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=4096" npm run build

# Continue on error and check exit code
- run: npm run build
  continue-on-error: true
  id: build
- if: steps.build.outcome == 'failure'
  run: echo "Build failed, investigating..."
```

title: Git error: fatal: The current branch has no upstream branch
body:
This error occurs when you try to push a new local branch that doesn't have a remote tracking branch set up.

```bash
# Error: fatal: The current branch feature has no upstream branch.
# To push the current branch and set the remote as upstream, use:
#   git push --set-upstream origin feature

# Fix: push with upstream tracking
git push -u origin feature
# or
git push --set-upstream origin feature

# Make this automatic for all new branches
git config --global push.autoSetupRemote true
# Now 'git push' automatically sets upstream on first push

# Check current upstream
git branch -vv
# * feature  abc1234 [origin/feature] Latest commit
# main       def5678 [origin/main] Main branch

# Change upstream
git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/main feature
```

Setting `push.autoSetupRemote true` (Git 2.37+) is recommended. It eliminates this error entirely by automatically creating the remote tracking branch on first push.

title: Git error: cannot lock ref — reference already exists
body:
This error usually means a stale lock file or a branch/tag name conflict.

```bash
# Error: cannot lock ref 'refs/heads/feature': reference already exists
# or: unable to create '.git/refs/heads/feature.lock': File exists

# Cause 1: Stale lock file (from interrupted git operation)
find .git -name '*.lock' -delete  # remove all lock files
# Or remove specific lock:
rm .git/refs/heads/feature.lock

# Cause 2: Branch name conflicts with path
# If 'feature' exists, you can't create 'feature/login'
# because 'feature' can't be both a ref and a directory
git branch feature       # exists
git branch feature/login # Error! Can't create directory 'feature' because file exists

# Fix: rename the conflicting branch
git branch -m feature feature-main
git branch feature/login  # now works

# Cause 3: Remote tracking refs are stale
git remote prune origin  # clean up stale remote refs
```

The branch naming conflict (feature vs feature/login) is the most common cause. Establish a naming convention: always use `feature/name`, never bare `feature`.

title: Git commit message convention with conventional commits
body:
Use conventional commits for automated changelogs, semantic versioning, and clear history.

```bash
# Format: type(scope): description
#
# Types:
# feat: new feature (MINOR version bump)
# fix: bug fix (PATCH version bump)
# docs: documentation only
# style: formatting, no code change
# refactor: code change that neither fixes nor adds
# perf: performance improvement
# test: adding or updating tests
# chore: build process, deps, tooling
# ci: CI configuration changes

# Examples:
git commit -m "feat(auth): add OAuth2 login with Google"
git commit -m "fix(api): handle null response from payment gateway"
git commit -m "docs: update API endpoint documentation"
git commit -m "refactor(db): extract query builder into separate module"

# Breaking change:
git commit -m "feat(api)!: change response format for /users endpoint

BREAKING CHANGE: The /users endpoint now returns paginated results.
Old format: { users: [...] }
New format: { data: [...], meta: { page, total } }"
```

Enforce with commitlint:

```bash
npm install -D @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional
echo 'module.exports = { extends: ["@commitlint/config-conventional"] }' > commitlint.config.js
# In .husky/commit-msg:
npx --no -- commitlint --edit $1
```

title: Git branch workflow: trunk-based development with short-lived branches
body:
Keep branches short-lived (1-3 days) and merge to main frequently. This reduces merge conflicts and keeps the codebase stable.

```bash
# Start work
git checkout main
git pull
git checkout -b feature/add-search

# Work on the feature (rebase from main frequently)
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main

# Multiple small commits during development
git commit -m "feat(search): add search input component"
git commit -m "feat(search): implement fuzzy matching algorithm"
git commit -m "test(search): add search integration tests"

# Before creating PR: clean up commits
git rebase -i origin/main  # squash WIP commits, reword messages

# Push and create PR
git push -u origin feature/add-search
gh pr create --title "feat: add search functionality" --body "..."

# After merge: clean up
git checkout main
git pull
git branch -d feature/add-search
```

Rules: no branch lives longer than 3 days, all work goes through PRs, main is always deployable, feature flags hide incomplete features.

title: GitHub Actions reusable workflow pattern for DRY CI/CD
body:
Create reusable workflows to share CI/CD logic across repos or reduce duplication within a repo.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/reusable-test.yml
name: Reusable Test Workflow
on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      node-version:
        type: string
        default: '22'
      working-directory:
        type: string
        default: '.'
    secrets:
      NPM_TOKEN:
        required: false

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run lint
```

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml — caller workflow
name: CI
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test-frontend:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-test.yml
    with:
      working-directory: packages/frontend
      node-version: '22'

  test-backend:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-test.yml
    with:
      working-directory: packages/backend
    secrets:
      NPM_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
```

Reusable workflows use `workflow_call` trigger. They can accept `inputs` and `secrets`. Call them with `uses:` pointing to the workflow file path.

title: Git pre-push hook for preventing force pushes and broken tests
body:
Guard against common mistakes with a pre-push hook that runs checks before pushing.

```bash
#!/bin/sh
# .husky/pre-push (or .githooks/pre-push)

# Get the remote and branch being pushed to
remote="$1"
url="$2"

# Prevent force push to main/production
while read local_ref local_oid remote_ref remote_oid; do
  if echo "$remote_ref" | grep -qE 'refs/heads/(main|master|production)'; then
    if [ "$(git merge-base $local_oid $remote_oid)" != "$remote_oid" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: Force push to protected branch detected!"
      echo "Branch: $remote_ref"
      echo "Use a PR instead."
      exit 1
    fi
  fi
done

# Run tests before push (only if test files changed)
changed_files=$(git diff --name-only HEAD~1)
if echo "$changed_files" | grep -qE '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$'; then
  echo "Running tests before push..."
  npm test -- --bail 2>/dev/null
  if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Tests failed. Fix before pushing."
    exit 1
  fi
fi

# Check for console.log/debugger left in code
if git diff --cached --name-only | grep -qE '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$'; then
  if git diff HEAD~1 | grep -q 'console\.log\|debugger'; then
    echo "WARNING: console.log or debugger found in diff"
    echo "Continue anyway? (set GIT_SKIP_HOOKS=1 to bypass)"
  fi
fi

exit 0
```

title: GitHub Actions composite action for reusable steps
body:
Composite actions package multiple steps into a single reusable action. Unlike reusable workflows, they run within the calling job.

```yaml
# .github/actions/setup-project/action.yml
name: Setup Project
description: Install dependencies and setup environment
inputs:
  node-version:
    description: Node.js version
    default: '22'
  install-playwright:
    description: Install Playwright browsers
    default: 'false'

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      with:
        node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
        cache: npm

    - name: Install dependencies
      shell: bash
      run: npm ci

    - name: Install Playwright
      if: inputs.install-playwright == 'true'
      shell: bash
      run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium

    - name: Build
      shell: bash
      run: npm run build
```

```yaml
# Usage in workflow
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-project
        with:
          install-playwright: 'true'
      - run: npm test
```

Composite actions must specify `shell:` for every `run:` step. They share the job's environment, filesystem, and service containers with the calling workflow.

title: Git conflict resolution workflow with three-way merge
body:
A systematic approach to resolving merge conflicts using the three-way merge strategy.

```bash
# Start the merge/rebase
git merge feature  # or git rebase main

# See all conflicted files
git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U

# For each conflicted file, understand the three versions:
git show :1:path/to/file  # common ancestor (base)
git show :2:path/to/file  # current branch (ours)
git show :3:path/to/file  # incoming branch (theirs)

# Use a merge tool
git mergetool  # opens configured merge tool
# or
git mergetool --tool=vimdiff

# Manual resolution: edit the conflict markers
<<<<<<< HEAD (ours)
const timeout = 5000;
=======
const timeout = 10000;
>>>>>>> feature (theirs)
# Remove markers and keep the correct version:
const timeout = 10000;

# After resolving all conflicts:
git add .  # mark as resolved
git merge --continue  # or git rebase --continue

# If you want to abort:
git merge --abort  # or git rebase --abort
```

Configure VS Code as merge tool: `git config --global merge.tool vscode && git config --global mergetool.vscode.cmd 'code --wait $MERGED'`

title: GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with test, build, and deploy stages
body:
A complete CI/CD pipeline with job dependencies, caching, and environment deployments.

```yaml
name: CI/CD Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 22, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npx tsc --noEmit

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: testdb
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 22, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test -- --coverage
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:test@localhost:5432/testdb
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: coverage
          path: coverage/

  build:
    needs: [lint, test]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 22, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: build
          path: dist/

  deploy:
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with: { name: build }
      - run: echo "Deploy to production"
```

title: Git monorepo workflow with sparse checkout and path-based triggers
body:
Efficient Git workflows for monorepos using sparse checkout and GitHub Actions path filters.

```bash
# Sparse checkout: only download specific directories
git clone --filter=blob:none --sparse <url>
cd repo
git sparse-checkout set packages/frontend
# Only packages/frontend is checked out

# Add more directories later
git sparse-checkout add packages/shared

# List sparse checkout paths
git sparse-checkout list
```

```yaml
# .github/workflows/frontend.yml — only runs for frontend changes
name: Frontend CI
on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'packages/frontend/**'
      - 'packages/shared/**'
      - 'package.json'
      - '.github/workflows/frontend.yml'
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'packages/frontend/**'
      - 'packages/shared/**'

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 22, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run test --workspace=packages/frontend
```

Path filters prevent unnecessary CI runs. Include shared dependency paths (like `packages/shared/**`) to catch breaking changes. Always include the workflow file itself in the paths filter so workflow changes trigger a run.

title: GitHub Actions deployment with environment protection rules
body:
Use GitHub environments for deployment approvals, secrets scoping, and deployment tracking.

```yaml
jobs:
  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: staging
      url: https://staging.example.com
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./deploy.sh staging
        env:
          DEPLOY_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DEPLOY_TOKEN }}

  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://example.com
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./deploy.sh production
        env:
          DEPLOY_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PROD_DEPLOY_TOKEN }}
```

In GitHub UI, configure each environment with:
- **Required reviewers**: 1-6 people who must approve before deployment
- **Wait timer**: delay (minutes) before deployment starts
- **Branch restrictions**: only allow deployments from specific branches
- **Environment secrets**: secrets scoped to that environment only

Secrets set on the `production` environment are only available to jobs that specify `environment: production`. This prevents accidental production deploys from feature branches.

title: Git alias configuration for common workflows
body:
Set up Git aliases to speed up common operations and reduce typing errors.

```bash
# Add these to ~/.gitconfig or run git config --global
git config --global alias.co 'checkout'
git config --global alias.br 'branch'
git config --global alias.ci 'commit'
git config --global alias.st 'status -sb'
git config --global alias.lg 'log --oneline --graph --decorate -20'
git config --global alias.lga 'log --oneline --graph --decorate --all -30'

# Useful compound aliases
git config --global alias.undo 'reset --soft HEAD~1'
git config --global alias.amend 'commit --amend --no-edit'
git config --global alias.unstage 'reset HEAD --'
git config --global alias.last 'log -1 HEAD --stat'
git config --global alias.fresh 'fetch --all --prune'

# Show what you worked on today
git config --global alias.today 'log --since=midnight --author="$(git config user.name)" --oneline'

# Delete merged branches
git config --global alias.cleanup '!git branch --merged main | grep -v main | xargs git branch -d'

# Interactive rebase last N commits
git config --global alias.ri '!f() { git rebase -i HEAD~${1:-5}; }; f'
```

```bash
# Usage:
git st        # git status -sb
git lg        # pretty log
git undo      # undo last commit, keep changes staged
git cleanup   # delete branches merged into main
git ri 3      # interactive rebase last 3 commits
git today     # show today's commits
```

title: GitHub Actions automatic PR labeling and size checking
body:
Automatically label PRs based on changed files and flag oversized PRs for review.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/pr-labeler.yml
name: PR Labeler
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  label:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Label by file paths
        uses: actions/labeler@v5
        with:
          repo-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Check PR size
        run: |
          additions=$(gh pr view ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --json additions -q '.additions')
          deletions=$(gh pr view ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --json deletions -q '.deletions')
          total=$((additions + deletions))

          if [ $total -gt 1000 ]; then
            gh pr edit ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --add-label "size/XL"
            gh pr comment ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --body \
              "⚠️ This PR has $total changed lines. Consider breaking it into smaller PRs."
          elif [ $total -gt 500 ]; then
            gh pr edit ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --add-label "size/L"
          elif [ $total -gt 100 ]; then
            gh pr edit ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --add-label "size/M"
          else
            gh pr edit ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --add-label "size/S"
          fi
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
```

```yaml
# .github/labeler.yml (config for actions/labeler)
frontend:
  - changed-files:
    - any-glob-to-any-file: ['src/components/**', '*.css', '*.tsx']
backend:
  - changed-files:
    - any-glob-to-any-file: ['src/api/**', 'src/db/**']
docs:
  - changed-files:
    - any-glob-to-any-file: ['docs/**', '*.md']
```

title: Git stash workflow for context switching without losing work
body:
A disciplined stash workflow for managing multiple work-in-progress contexts.

```bash
# Save current work with descriptive message
git stash push -u -m "feat: login form — halfway through validation"

# Do the urgent thing
git checkout main
git checkout -b hotfix/critical-bug
# ... fix the bug ...
git commit -m "fix: critical security patch"
git push -u origin hotfix/critical-bug

# Return to original work
git checkout feature/login
git stash list
# stash@{0}: On feature/login: feat: login form — halfway through validation

git stash pop  # apply and remove from stash

# Advanced: stash specific files
git stash push -m "stash only the hook" -- src/hooks/useAuth.ts

# Save stash for longer term (like a named branch but lighter)
git stash push -u -m "experiment: alternative search algorithm"
# Come back days later:
git stash list  # find it
git stash apply stash@{2}  # apply without removing

# Drop old stashes
git stash drop stash@{3}
git stash clear  # delete ALL stashes (careful!)
```

Always use `-m` with a descriptive message. Without messages, stashes pile up and become unidentifiable. Treat stash as a short-term holding area, not long-term storage — for that, use branches.

title: GitHub Actions workflow for automated releases with semantic versioning
body:
Automatically create releases, bump versions, and generate changelogs from conventional commits.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Release
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - run: npm ci

      - name: Create release
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
        run: npx semantic-release
```

```json
// .releaserc.json
{
  "branches": ["main"],
  "plugins": [
    "@semantic-release/commit-analyzer",
    "@semantic-release/release-notes-generator",
    "@semantic-release/changelog",
    ["@semantic-release/npm", { "npmPublish": true }],
    ["@semantic-release/github", {
      "assets": ["dist/**"]
    }],
    ["@semantic-release/git", {
      "assets": ["CHANGELOG.md", "package.json"],
      "message": "chore(release): ${nextRelease.version}"
    }]
  ]
}
```

Semantic-release reads commit messages to determine the version bump: `fix:` = patch, `feat:` = minor, `BREAKING CHANGE:` = major. The changelog is generated automatically from commit messages.

title: GitHub Actions workflow for Docker build with layer caching
body:
Build and push Docker images efficiently using GitHub Actions with build cache.

```yaml
name: Docker Build
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        id: meta
        with:
          images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}
          tags: |
            type=ref,event=branch
            type=ref,event=pr
            type=semver,pattern={{version}}
            type=sha

      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: ${{ github.event_name != 'pull_request' }}
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
```

The `cache-from/cache-to: type=gha` uses GitHub Actions cache for Docker layers. The `mode=max` caches all layers, not just the final image layers. This dramatically speeds up subsequent builds.

title: GitHub Actions workflow for running Playwright E2E tests
body:
Run Playwright end-to-end tests in CI with artifacts for failed test screenshots.

```yaml
name: E2E Tests
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  e2e:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm

      - run: npm ci

      - name: Install Playwright browsers
        run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium

      - name: Build app
        run: npm run build

      - name: Run E2E tests
        run: npx playwright test --project=chromium
        env:
          CI: true
          BASE_URL: http://localhost:3000

      - name: Upload test results
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: playwright-report
          path: playwright-report/
          retention-days: 7

      - name: Upload screenshots
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: test-screenshots
          path: test-results/
          retention-days: 7
```

Install only the browsers you need (`--with-deps chromium` instead of all browsers) to reduce CI time. Use `if: failure()` for artifacts so you only upload when tests fail.

title: Git advanced log commands for investigation and blame
body:
Power-user Git log commands for tracking down changes, understanding history, and investigating issues.

```bash
# Find who last modified each line of a file
git blame src/utils/auth.ts
git blame -L 10,30 src/utils/auth.ts  # specific line range
git blame --since="3.weeks" src/utils/auth.ts  # only recent changes

# Find when a specific line was added
git log -S "connectToDatabase" --oneline  # search for string in diffs
git log -G "timeout.*=.*\d+" --oneline     # regex search in diffs

# Show all changes to a specific function
git log -L :functionName:src/utils/auth.ts

# Show commits that touched a specific file
git log --follow -- src/utils/auth.ts  # --follow tracks renames

# Find merge commit that introduced a change
git log --merges --ancestry-path abc1234..HEAD

# Graph view of branch topology
git log --graph --oneline --all --decorate -20

# What did I do last week?
git log --author="$(git config user.name)" --since="1 week ago" --oneline

# Show files changed in each commit
git log --stat --oneline -10

# Show diff for each commit (verbose)
git log -p -5 -- src/utils/auth.ts

# Find deleted files
git log --diff-filter=D --summary | grep 'delete mode'
```

title: GitHub Actions matrix with service containers for database testing
body:
Test against multiple database versions using GitHub Actions service containers.

```yaml
name: Database Tests
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - db: postgres
            db-version: '15'
            db-port: 5432
          - db: postgres
            db-version: '16'
            db-port: 5432
          - db: mysql
            db-version: '8.0'
            db-port: 3306

    services:
      postgres:
        image: ${{ matrix.db == 'postgres' && format('postgres:{0}', matrix.db-version) || '' }}
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: test
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: testdb
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

      mysql:
        image: ${{ matrix.db == 'mysql' && format('mysql:{0}', matrix.db-version) || '' }}
        env:
          MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: test
          MYSQL_DATABASE: testdb
        ports: ['3306:3306']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "mysqladmin ping"
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 22, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
        env:
          DB_TYPE: ${{ matrix.db }}
          DB_PORT: ${{ matrix.db-port }}
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ matrix.db == 'postgres' && 'postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/testdb' || 'mysql://root:test@localhost:3306/testdb' }}
```

title: Git patch workflow for sharing changes without pushing
body:
Create and apply patches to share changes over email, chat, or between disconnected repos.

```bash
# Create a patch from the last commit
git format-patch -1 HEAD
# Creates: 0001-commit-message.patch

# Create patches from the last 3 commits
git format-patch -3

# Create a patch from a branch diff
git format-patch main..feature
# Creates one .patch file per commit

# Create a single combined diff
git diff main..feature > changes.diff

# Apply a patch (preserves commit info)
git am 0001-commit-message.patch
git am *.patch  # apply all patches in order

# Apply a raw diff
git apply changes.diff

# Check if a patch applies cleanly
git apply --check changes.diff

# If patch fails to apply:
git am --abort  # cancel
# or
git am --3way  # try 3-way merge for conflicts

# Create a patch from staged changes only
git diff --cached > staged-changes.patch
```

Patches are useful for: contributing to projects where you don't have push access, sharing changes in code review comments, and porting changes between repos that don't share a remote.

title: GitHub Actions workflow for dependency updates with automated PRs
body:
Automatically create PRs when dependencies have updates, with auto-merge for patch versions.

```yaml
# .github/dependabot.yml
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: npm
    directory: /
    schedule:
      interval: weekly
      day: monday
      time: '09:00'
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
    groups:
      dev-dependencies:
        dependency-type: development
        update-types: [minor, patch]
      production-dependencies:
        dependency-type: production
        update-types: [patch]
    reviewers:
      - team-lead
    labels:
      - dependencies
    commit-message:
      prefix: chore(deps)
```

```yaml
# .github/workflows/auto-merge-dependabot.yml
name: Auto-merge Dependabot
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  auto-merge:
    if: github.actor == 'dependabot[bot]'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Auto-merge patch updates
        run: |
          gh pr merge --auto --squash ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
```

Group related dependencies together so they're updated in a single PR. Auto-merge patch updates but require manual review for minor/major versions.
